messages.content:
You are a GDPR compliance analyst. When given a document excerpt, evaluate it step by step against each GDPR requirement. For each step, quote the relevant text if found, or state it is missing. End with a structured verdict listing all violations. Always follow this exact format:

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): ...
Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): ...
Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): ...
Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): ...
Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): ...
Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): ...
Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): ...
Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): ...
Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): ...
Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): ...

VERDICT: [Compliant / Non-Compliant / Partially Compliant]
VIOLATIONS: [list each article breached]
RECOMMENDATIONS: [one line per fix needed]

Assess the following HR background check consent form for GDPR compliance:

"As part of our hiring process, we will conduct a background check including criminal record verification, employment history, and educational credentials. By signing below, you provide explicit consent for this processing. Results will be shared only with the hiring team. Records are retained for 1 year if unsuccessful, or for the duration of employment if hired. You may withdraw consent prior to the check being initiated by notifying HR in writing."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [PARTIAL] Consent is stated, but criminal record checks may require a stronger basis. In an employment context, consent may not be freely given due to power imbalance.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [FAIL] Criminal record data is governed by Art. 10, not Art. 9, and requires specific domestic legal authorisation. Merely obtaining consent is insufficient for criminal record processing.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PASS] Three purposes clearly stated: criminal verification, employment history, educational credentials.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PASS] Scope limited to background check data relevant to the role.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [PASS] 1 year for unsuccessful candidates; employment duration for hired candidates. Both clearly stated.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [PARTIAL] Consent withdrawal prior to check is addressed. Post-check rights not stated.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [PARTIAL] Results shared with hiring team only is stated, but the background check provider is a processor and is not disclosed.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [FAIL] Not addressed. Background check providers may process data in non-EEA jurisdictions.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [FAIL] Not addressed.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [FAIL] Not addressed.

VERDICT: Non-Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 10 (criminal record processing requires domestic legal authorisation, not just consent), Art. 6 (consent may not be freely given in employment context), Art. 15-21 (post-check rights absent), Art. 28 (background check provider not disclosed), Art. 46 (transfer safeguards absent)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Replace consent with a legally authorised basis for criminal record checks under applicable national law
- Disclose the background check provider as a data processor with a DPA reference
- Add post-check data subject rights section
- Address potential international transfers by the background check provider
- Confirm DPO requirement
